Inclusion in education
======================

Leaving no learner behind

UNESCO believes that every learner matters equally. Yet millions of people worldwide continue to be excluded from education for reasons which might include gender, sexual orientation, ethnic or social origin, language, religion, nationality, economic condition or ability. Inclusive education works to identify all barriers to education and remove them and covers everything from curricula to pedagogy and teaching. UNESCO’s work in this area is firstly guided by the [UNESCO Convention against Discrimination in Education (1960)](https://www.unesco.org/en/legal-affairs/convention-against-discrimination-education?hub=70285) as well as Sustainable Development Goal 4 and the [Education 2030 Framework for Action](https://unesdoc.unesco.org/images/0024/002456/245656e.pdf) which emphasize inclusion and equity as the foundation for quality education.

# Q&A: How inclusion in education has evolved

![inclusion in education](/sites/default/files/styles/paragraph_medium_desktop/article/2024-03/inclusion-in-education-q-and-a.jpg?itok=bT8T9L5b)

### Summary

## What is the Salamanca Statement on Inclusion in Education?

The [Salamanca Statement on Inclusion in Education](https://unesdoc.unesco.org/ark:/48223/pf0000098427) was adopted at the joint UNESCO and Ministry of Education of Spain World Conference on Special Needs Education held in Salamanca, Spain, in 1994. The Statement and its accompanying framework for action represent a worldwide consensus on future directions for the education of children with special needs. It is considered as having provided the impetus for inclusive education worldwide.

The Salamanca Statement emphasized the principle of inclusive education, which advocates for the education of all children, regardless of their physical, intellectual, social, emotional, linguistic, or other conditions, in schools. It called on governments to adopt inclusive policies and stressed the importance of adapting teaching methods, curricula and environments to accommodate the needs of all students. It highlighted the need for adequate training and support for teachers to enable them to address the diverse needs of students effectively and emphasized the importance of collaboration among governments, non-governmental organizations, communities and families to promote inclusive education.

## What has happened since the adoption of this Statement?

Since the Salamanca Statement, efforts for inclusive education have continued. The Sustainable Development Goals adopted in 2015 and specifically Goal 4 recognizes the urgent need to provide equitable and inclusive quality education for all learners from the early years through compulsory schooling, technical and vocational education and training, higher education and lifelong learning. It calls on governments to step up efforts to achieve inclusion in education with special emphasis on the role of civil society and the involvement of marginalized groups. It was further highlighted at the United Nations [Transforming Education Summit](https://www.unesco.org/en/2022-transforming-education-summit) (TES) in 2022, which recalled the baseline of the Sustainable Development Goal – leave no one behind.

Many countries have enacted laws and policies to promote inclusive education. Of the 133 national commitments to transform education made by countries at the TES, 87% recognized the importance of ensuring more inclusive education systems that cater for the needs of the most vulnerable learners and communities.

Despite progress since the Salamanca Statement, deep disparities remain. [Millions of children and youth are still denied their right to education](https://education-estimates.org/out-of-school/). Over 7 million refugee children are not enrolled in school. Poverty, geographical location, gender, language, disability, ethnicity, migration or displacement status continue to dictate and limit education opportunities.

There has been a gradual shift in attitudes towards inclusion in education. More stakeholders, including educators, policymakers, parents and communities are recognizing the value of inclusive education in promoting diversity, equity and social cohesion. [Data shows](https://www.education-inequalities.org/indicators/comp_prim_v2#maxYear=2021&minYear=2016&ageGroup=%22comp_prim_v2%22&dimension=%7B%22id%22%3A%22disability%22%2C%22filters%22%3A%5B%5D%7D) that when children have at least one sensory difficulty, they are less likely to complete primary school than children who have no sensory difficulties.

## What are the positive trends to ensure inclusion in education?

There is growing emphasis on providing teachers with training and professional development opportunities to effectively support inclusive practices in the classroom. Educators are learning strategies for differentiating instruction, fostering inclusive classroom environments and collaborating with special education professionals and support staff. However, [around 40% of countries still do not provide teacher training on inclusion](https://unesdoc.unesco.org/ark:/48223/pf0000374447).

The development and integration of assistive technologies have expanded opportunities for students with disabilities to access educational materials and participate in learning activities alongside their peers. Yet, [laws in 25% of countries make provisions for educating children with disabilities in separate settings, rising to over 40% in Asia and in Latin America and the Caribbean](https://education-profiles.org/themes/~inclusion).

There is increasing recognition of the intersectionality of identities and experiences among students. Inclusive education efforts are evolving to address the unique needs and challenges faced by students who belong to multiple marginalized groups, such as students with disabilities who are also from minority ethnic or linguistic backgrounds.[In at least 20 countries, hardly any poor, rural young woman complete upper secondary school.](https://www.education-inequalities.org/)

Overall, the evolution of inclusion in education since the Salamanca Statement and the adoption of the Sustainable Development Goals in 2015 reflect a growing commitment to creating equitable, diverse and inclusive learning environments where every learner matters, and matters equally. Barriers to inclusion include poor attitudes, inaccessible and unsafe learning environments, rigid curriculum, ineffective teacher training, lack of knowledge of the language of instruction, socio-economic barriers to education, and lack of political will and funding.

## How is UNESCO supporting inclusion in education?

UNESCO plays a significant role in promoting inclusive education around the world through various initiatives, programmes and advocacy efforts. UNESCO supports governments to adopt inclusive education frameworks, provides technical assistance and builds capacity among governments, education stakeholders and institutions to develop inclusive education systems. The Global Education Monitoring Report’s [SCOPE](https://www.education-progress.org/en/articles/access) and [VIEW](https://education-estimates.org/) databases track indicators related to access, participation, quality and equity in education, including the inclusion of marginalized and vulnerable populations.

More work remains to be done. UNESCO calls on governments and partners to support inclusive education by:

# What you need to know about inclusion in education

What you need to know about inclusion in education

Last update:21 February 2025

## Why does UNESCO consider inclusion in education important?

Despite significant progress in the last decade, millions are still denied their right to education and learning opportunities continue to be unequally distributed. Globally, one in five children, adolescents and youth, are entirely excluded from education. Poverty, location, gender, language, disability, ethnicity, religion, migration or displacement status are among factors that continue to dictate and limit opportunities. [More than a quarter of a billion learners lack education in the language that they understand best](https://unesdoc.unesco.org/ark:/48223/pf0000392477), and children with disabilities continue to be disproportionally excluded from school. Three quarters of all primary-age children - 9 million- who may never set foot in school are girls.

Moreover, since 2000, an upsurge in migration and displacement has led to a 26% increase of migrant and refugee children globally, making their inclusion in national education systems an imperative. Before the Covid-19 pandemic, 20 per cent of children and young people faced exclusion from education on a daily basis. The crisis has made some inequalities more visible, widened existing disparities and led to new inequalities and exclusion in education particularly for marginalized and disadvantaged groups. Some 40% of the poorest countries could not support learners at risk during the pandemic, further demonstrating the fragility of the right to education.

## How does inclusion relate to the right to education?

The right to education aims to ensure everyone achieves their human right to access quality education throughout life. An inclusive approach to education means that each individual’s needs are taken into account and that all learners participate and achieve together. It acknowledges that all children can learn and that every child has unique characteristics, interests, abilities and learning needs. Special focus is placed on learners who may be at risk of marginalization, exclusion or underachievement. For example, where a child has a disability he or she would not be separated from other learners in school and learning assessments and progress would take the disability into account.

## ​​​​​​​How does UNESCO work to ensure inclusion in education?

UNESCO focuses on the inclusivity of the whole education system rather than trying to remove barriers one by one; the emphasis is on how to transform existing systems rather than on how some learners can be integrated into them. It promotes education systems that are based on gender equality, that respect diverse needs, abilities and characteristics and eliminate all forms of discrimination in the learning environment. UNESCO helps Member States develop and implement inclusive policies and programmes which reach excluded and marginalized groups and provide them with quality education and helps governments and partners translate policy into inclusive curricula, pedagogy and teaching and programme design and delivery. Among marginalized and vulnerable groups, UNESCO pays special attention to children with disabilities as they are overrepresented in the population of those who are not in education. Indigenous people also continue to experience exclusion within and from education.

## ​​​​​​​Why is language diversity important?

Learners may be excluded if learning is conducted in something other than their mother tongue. In addition, multilingual education based on the mother tongue (s) in the early years of schooling plays a key role in fostering respect for diversity and a sense of interconnectedness between countries and populations. Yet [linquistic diversity](https://www.un.org/ga/search/view_doc.asp?symbol=A/RES/56/262&Lang=E) is increasingly threatened as more and more languages disappear. Globally [more than a quarter of a billion learners lack education in the language that they understand best](https://unesdoc.unesco.org/ark:/48223/pf0000392477). Multilingual and multicultural societies exist through their languages which also transmit and preserve traditional knowledge and cultures in a sustainable way.

## What makes an inclusive learning environment?

There are many aspects to be considered in creating an inclusive learning environment including the development of policy with an inclusive lens, adequate and disaggregated data about learners, curriculum, teacher ability and attitudes, language and communication, assistive technology, physical access including transport, and community and family involvement. Children with disabilities are over-represented in the population of those who are not in education. Globally, there are between 93 million and 150 million children living with a disability – 80 per cent of whom live in developing countries. Children and youth with sensory, physical, or learning disabilities are two-and-a-half times more likely than their peers to never go to school. Where disability intersects with other barriers, such as gender, poverty, or remoteness, the risk of exclusion is greater still.

## What is the Cali Commitment to equity and inclusion in education?

At the International Forum on Inclusion and Equity in Cali, Colombia in 2019 young people,  government officials, educators, civil society, and multilateral organizations representatives pledged themselves to the [Cali Commitment](https://unesdoc.unesco.org/ark:/48223/pf0000370910), a reaffirmation of the international human rights agenda reflected in the Sustainable Development Goals and the Education 2030 Framework for Action, which recognizes the urgent need to provide equitable and inclusive quality education for all learners, from the early years through compulsory schooling, technical and vocational education and training, higher education, and lifelong learning. It calls on governments to step up efforts to achieve inclusion in education with special emphasis on the role of civil society and the involvement of marginalized groups.

In line with its mandate, the 2020 GEM Report assesses progress towards Sustainable Development Goal 4 (SDG 4) on education and its ten targets, as well as other related education targets in the SDG agenda. The report also addresses inclusion in education, drawing attention to all those excluded from education, because of background or ability. The report is motivated by the explicit reference to inclusion in the 2015 Incheon Declaration, and the call to ensure an inclusive and equitable quality education in the formulation of SDG 4, the global goal for education. It reminds us that, no matter what argument may be built to the contrary, we have a moral imperative to ensure every child has a right to an appropriate education of high quality.

The report also explores the challenges holding us back from achieving this vision and demonstrates concrete policy examples from countries managing to tackle them with success. These include differing understandings of the word inclusion, lack of teacher support, absence of data on those excluded from education, inappropriate infrastructure, persistence of parallel systems and special schools, lack of political will and community support, untargeted finance, uncoordinated governance, multiple but inconsistent laws, and policies that are not being followed through.

Alongside the report, an online database demonstrates education inequalities, the [Worldwide Inequality Database on Education Inequalities (WIDE)](https://www.unesco.org/gem-report/en/publication/www.education-inequalities.org). [The Profiles Enhancing Education Reviews (PEER)](https://education-profiles.org/themes/~inclusion) platform prepared by the GEM Report describes countries’ laws and policies on inclusion and education.

Two special regional reports produced in collaboration with regional partners deep dive into inclusion and education in Latin America and the Caribbean and in Central and Eastern Europe, Caucasus and Central Asia.

ABSTRACT
--------

This article draws upon a critical policy analysis approach to examine the state of inclusive education policy in Global North settings over the past decade. Building on an earlier paper on this topic ten years ago, this updated article seeks to explore whether and how inclusion and inclusive education have been understood in varied international, national and sub-national policy settings over time. While it might be anticipated that schooling systems should be more aware and proactive in supporting inclusion within their policies, our findings reveal mixed results. At times, there appears to have been regression in some settings, stagnation in others, as well as moderate progress in other settings in relation to support and advocacy for inclusion in educational policies. In an era of increased attention to a range of issues of inclusion more broadly over the past decade (e.g. marriage equality, gender fluidity, Black Lives Matter, #StopAsianHate), our article cautions against assuming that such movements have somehow led to a more ‘inclusive’ conception of students’ identities and wellbeing in schooling policy. Whether and how teachers can be expected to be more inclusive in their practices in such a variegated policy environment is an area for continued inquiry.

Introduction
------------

This paper is a follow-up to an earlier article written a decade ago about the state of inclusive education policies in predominantly Global North settings (see Hardy & Woodcock, [Citation 2015](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)). We drew upon a range of international policies from UNESCO (including the _Salamanca Statement and Framework for Action on Special Needs Education_ (the ‘Salamanca Agreement’)), OECD (including various _Education at a Glance_ reports from 2012 and 2013), as well as relevant national and sub-national policies from the USA, Canada (including Ontario), England and Australia (including New South Wales and Queensland). The original article revealed a variety of discourses of diversity but also deficit. Given it has been almost ten years since that article was published, and that this has been a period of increasing recognition of diversity more broadly in society (e.g. marriage equality, gender fluidity, Black Lives Matter, #StopAsianHate), we believe it is important and necessary to see whether and how effectively a more inclusive ethos has permeated schooling policies since that time. Do more recent education policies reflect progress over the past decade?

In this article, we revisit the key themes of that paper to see whether and how well they have ‘travelled’ over time. We are conscious that issues of inclusion are expressed differently in different contexts. We also acknowledge that the original paper focused only on Western settings ‘with a long history of advocacy and support (at least at the discursive level) for issues of inclusion’ (Authors 2015, 144). Given our interest in issues of change (or lack of change) over time, this paper again focuses upon the international, national and sub-national settings referred to in the original piece.

We commence by providing an overview of a critical policy analytical approach, and how such an analysis pertains to issues of inclusion. We then elaborate the specific data collection and methods we deployed to identify relevant policies and policy artefacts. This is followed by an overview of the key national/sub-national (and relevant international) policies in each of the key Global North jurisdictions identified in the research. We analyse these policies in light of whether and how inclusion and inclusive education have been understood in these varied international, national and sub-national policy settings over time. We reveal both consistencies and inconsistencies over time, with more and less inclusive discourses evident within and across contexts. We conclude with an elaboration of the implications of the research, and necessary next steps.

Analysing inclusion: a critical policy analysis approach
--------------------------------------------------------

As with the earlier paper, we again draw upon a broadly critical policy sociology approach – an approach that contests more traditional understandings of policy analysis that seek to find ‘solutions’ to particular ‘problems’ at hand (Ball [Citation 1994](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#), [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#); Taylor et al. [Citation 1997](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)). While more conservative policy analysis approaches frame policy development and analysis as a more clearly defined and staged process of problem identification and resolution, more critical policy approaches frame policy texts and associated documents as inherently political in orientation and reflective of the advantaging and disadvantaging of particular groups. Such an approach is cognisant of not simply the policy documents or texts themselves but also the broader background and context of policies, as well as their effects or consequences (Taylor et al. [Citation 1997](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)). The histories of policies are also an important part of understanding any given policy and its effects.

Policy research necessarily involves recognising the contestation and multiplicity of ideas and the complex ways in which key ideas reflect existing power structures. Policies are always overlaid by broader political influences that have effects:

Policy discourses, therefore, do not mirror reality or represent ‘political truth’, but are constructs that evolve from contested and power-laden processes of meaning-making. … Interpreting policy materials thus requires recognition of the partiality, contestability and mutability of ideas; and the complex ways ideas result from collective meaning-making processes that reflect existing power networks. (Savage [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#), 17)

Policies are also important for the work that they do in defining particular representations of the world and the subsequent governance processes they enable. In this sense, policy analysis is also a process of problem identification and elaboration. Bacchi’s ([Citation 2009](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)) formulation of ‘What's the problem represented to be?’ is particularly generative in this regard. On such a rendering, particular policy issues are presented as ‘problems’ and the way in which policy articulates such issues anticipates and projects particular ‘solutions’ and ‘understandings’ to such problems.
Importantly, sometimes policies are incoherent and perhaps absurd; they are far less rational than the descriptions and depictions often afforded them (Ball [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#); Webb [Citation 2014](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)). Taking seriously the ‘absurdity’ of policy entails not so much a ‘giving up’ of the possibilities and potentialities of policy but instead seeking to understand policies as inherently uncertain (Ball [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)) and working with such uncertainty on a continuing basis to help ‘make sense’ of policies and their inherent incoherences. Policies can be understood as permeable and fluid arrangements that enact strategic work in varied and sometimes problematic ways. It is useful therefore to identify and comprehend the irrationality and incoherence that attend policy and not simply construe policy as unambiguously rational.

Policy problematics also need to be seen as reproductive and exhibiting continuity (Ball [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)), even as they may be adverse in their effects over time. At the same time, policy incoherence can also be manifest in the form of an array of different policy initiatives supported as part of a broader policy ecology of previous policies and current ideas seen as necessary or possible ‘solutions’ to the broader problems at hand. As a result, ‘policy begets policy as new “solutions” are generated to respond to the failures, inadequacies and inefficacies of previous fixes multiplying incoherence’ (Ball [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#), 389). Under these circumstances, as Ball ([Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)) indicates, ‘single policy studies can miss this kind of mix and match effect’ (389), which becomes apparent if a more longitudinal approach is taken to policy analysis. It is in this spirit that we have sought to draw upon an array of relevant policies at present in varied national and international contexts, but which we endeavour to refer to in relation to an array of earlier policies which sought to exert influence within and across these contexts over time.

Finally, policy analysis is always necessarily an interpretivist undertaking, informed by analysts’ understandings of the world as it is, and as they would want it to be. Both authors are keenly aware of the need for much more robust approach to issues of inclusion and of the prejudices that characterise schooling practices and the need for a broader policy ecology to highlight and challenge such problematic practices. Focusing attention upon schooling policies is therefore an important part of a broader political project oriented towards more inclusive educational settings. This is particularly crucial within a wider educational context influenced by neoliberal approaches and foci which tend to marginalise conceptions of education that are not seen as readily contributing to overall economic productivity or which are not construed as sufficiently ‘efficient’ and ‘effective’ to warrant continued investment; broader global indicators and measures (such as PISA and associated standardised national measures) contribute to these omissions (Teodoro [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)). Waitoller ([Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)), drawing upon Brenner and Theodore’s ([Citation 2002](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)) notion of ‘actually existing neoliberalism’, flags how inclusion is diminished by such foci. Hedegaard-Soerensen and Penthin Grumloese ([Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)) reveal the effects of such exclusion in a (Danish) context dominated by focus upon achievement and results, and how in a context in which there is purported attention to both inclusion and achievement, the latter dominates over the former.

Methods
-------

To better understand issues of educational inclusion within their policy context, we draw upon key international, national and sub-national policies, and associated texts. We purposively identified Western settings with a substantive history of advocacy for inclusion (at least discursively) and prominent international bodies that seek to exert influence in education more broadly. The latter includes UNESCO and the OECD. Key policy guidelines from USA, Canada, England, and Australia were included. Given schooling is the responsibility of various sub-national entities in most of these jurisdictions, we also drew upon significant policies that claim to be ‘inclusive’ at the state/provincial level. Consequently, at a more localised level, we drew upon key policies within Ontario (as the largest province in Canada), and New South Wales and Queensland in Australia (as two large states that have taken reasonably divergent approaches to inclusion in the past).

Table 1 summarises the principal policies and policy artefacts/related documents drawn upon in this analysis. These policies were analysed in light of a critical policy analysis approach, revealing affordances and constraints, limitations, and areas of omission, and how these have changed over time.

Inclusion policy: contexts and texts
------------------------------------

### UNESCO

Since 2014, the range of additional UNESCO policies seeking to cultivate inclusion and inclusive education has been significant. While a decade ago, there was strong, explicit reference to the _Salamanca Agreement_ (UNESCO [Citation 1994](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)) almost exclusively, and even as the original _UNESCO Convention against Discrimination in Education_ ([Citation 1960](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)) continues to resonate, there is now a much wider range of foci and resources that help support inclusion for all, including Sustainable Goal 4 through the _Education 2030 Incheon Declaration and Framework for Action for the Implementation of Sustainable Goal 4_ (UNESCO [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#)). UNESCO is explicit about the importance of ‘Inclusion in Education’ in its website:

UNESCO believes that every learner matters equally. Yet millions of people worldwide continue to be excluded from education for reasons which might include sex, gender orientation, ethnic or social origin, language, religion, nationality, economic condition or ability. Inclusive education works to identify all barriers to education and remove them and covers everything from curricula to pedagogy and teaching. (UNESCO [Citation 2023](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13603116.2023.2257697#))

Such foci reflect overt inclusive expressions of education, beyond more neoliberal iterations.
At a macro level, such a statement reflects UNESCO’s support for the development of 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), which seek to foster peace and prosperity, enhanced partnerships between countries, removing poverty and enhancing health and education and reducing inequality, as well as enhancing economic growth b

enriched_points.expanded_content: There is an increasing global acknowledgment of the importance of inclusive education, ensuring equitable access to quality education for all learners, regardless of their backgrounds or abilities.
Recent studies involving 35 member countries highlight disparities in educational access, showcasing the urgent need for tailored policies to support marginalized and vulnerable student populations.

Inclusive education policies are increasingly aligned with UNESCO's frameworks, particularly the Education 2030 Agenda, emphasizing universal access and the elimination of barriers in education systems.
Tailored inclusive education policies are crucial for addressing diverse learner needs, promoting effective teaching strategies, and ensuring that no student is left behind in educational settings.
